title: Post Merger Integration
lead: Using ITOM to integrate IT landscapes, processes, and organisations after a merger or acquisition — aligning operating models and eliminating duplication.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: Post Merger Integration (PMI) is one of the most complex and time-critical scenarios for IT operating model redesign. When two organisations merge, their IT landscapes, processes, teams, contracts, and governance structures must be rationalised into a coherent whole. Without a deliberate ITOM approach, mergers result in shadow IT, duplicated capabilities, conflicting processes, and cultural friction that erode the value the merger was supposed to create.

sections.heading: ITOM Perspective
sections.body: In a PMI scenario, the ITOM serves as the integration blueprint. It defines the target state for the combined IT organisation: which processes are standardised, which platforms are consolidated, how governance is unified, and how teams from both organisations are restructured. The ITOM approach ensures that integration decisions are made holistically rather than system-by-system, preventing the 'integration spaghetti' that plagues many mergers. Speed matters — the longer the integration takes, the more value leaks.

items.desc: two sets of processes, tooling, and governance that must coexist during transition and converge to one
different working cultures, methodologies (Agile vs. waterfall), and decision-making styles
overlapping application landscapes that need to be consolidated without disrupting business continuity
different vendor contracts, SLAs, and licensing that must be renegotiated or consolidated
ensuring critical IT services work from the first day of the merged entity
preventing knowledge loss as teams are restructured and roles change
one organisation's operating model is adopted; the other migrates to it — fastest but highest change impact
cherry-pick the best processes, tools, and structures from both organisations — balanced but slower
design a new operating model from scratch for the merged entity — cleanest but most effort
maintain separate operating models with a thin integration layer — lowest disruption but limited synergy
PMI assessment — rapid analysis of both IT landscapes, processes, and organisational structures
Target operating model design — define the integrated ITOM for the merged entity
Integration roadmap — wave-based plan for converging platforms, processes, and teams
Day-1 planning — ensure critical service continuity from merger completion
Transformation management — change management and communication throughout integration
